div.head: The TragicalTragicall History of D. Faustus (1604)

sp.l: Not marching now in fields of ThrasimeneThracimene,
Where Mars did mate the Carthaginians,
Nor sporting in the dalliance of loveloue,
In courts of Kings where state is overturnedouerturnd,
Nor in the pomppompe of proudprowd audacious deedsdeedes,
Intends our Muse to daunt his heavenlyheauenly verse:
OnlyOnely this, (gentlemenGentlemen:) we must performperforme,
The formforme of Faustus' fortunes good or bad.
To patient JudgmentsIudgements we appealappeale our plaudplaude,
And speakspeake for Faustus in his infancyinfancie.:
Now is he borne, his parents base of stockstocke,
In Germany, within a towntowne calledcalld Rhodes:
Of riper yearsyeeres to Wertenberg he went,
Whereas his kinsmen chiefly brought him upvp;,
So soonsoone hetree profitsprofites in divinityDiuinitie,
The fruitfulfruitfull plot of scholarismScholerisme gracedgrac't,
That shortly he was gracedgrac't with doctor'sDoctors name,
Excelling all, whose sweetsweete delight disputes
In heavenlyheauenly matters of theologyTheologie,
'TilTill swollenswolne with cunning of a selfselfe conceit,
His waxen wings did mount aboveaboue his reach,
And, melting, heavensheauens conspiredconspirde his overthrowouerthrow;.
For falling to a devilishdiuelish exercise,
And glutted more with learning'slearnings golden gifts,
He surfeitssurffets uponvpon cursed necromancyNegromancy.,
Nothing so sweetsweete as magicmagicke is to him
Which he preferspreferres before his chiefest blissblisse.,
And this the man that in his study sits. Exit.

sp.speaker: Faustus
sp.l: Settle thy studies, Faustus, and beginbeginne
To sound the depthdeapth of that thou wilt professprofesse;:
HavingHauing commencedcommencde, be a divineDiuine in showshew,
Yet levelleuell at the end of everyeuery artArt,
And liveliue and die in Aristotle'sAristotles worksworkes.:
SweetSweete AnalyticsAnulatikes 'tistis thou hashast ravishedrauisht me:,
Is, to dispute well, Logic'sLogickes chiefest end?
AffordsAffoords this Art no greater miraclemyracle?:
Then readreade no more, thou hashast attainedattaind the end;:
A greater subjectsubiect fitteth Faustus'Faustus wit.,
Bid Oncaymaeon farewell;, Galen come:
Seeing, Ubivbi desinit philosophus, ibi incipit medicus,.
Be a physicianphysition Faustus, heapheape upvp goldgolde,
And be eternis'deternizde for some wondrous cure.,
The end of physicphysicke is our bodies health.:
Why, Faustus, hashast thou not attainedattaind that end?
Is not thy common talktalke sound aphorismsAphorismes?
Are not thy billsbilles hung upvp as monuments,
Wherebywhereby whole citiesCitties havehaue escapedescapt the plague,
And thousand desperatedesprate maladies beenbeene easedeasde?,
Yet art thou still but Faustus, and a man.
Wouldstwouldst thou make man to liveliue eternally?
Or, being dead, raise them to life againagaine?
Then this profession were to be esteemedesteemd.
PhysicPhysicke farewell., Wherewhere is JustinianIustinian?

sp.l: A pretty case of paltry legacies:
Such is the subjectsubiect of the institute
And universalvniuersall body of the Church.:
His study fitsfittes a mercenary drudge,
Whowho aimsaimes at nothing but externalexternall trash,
The devildeuill and illiberalilliberall for me :
Whenwhen all is done, divinityDiuinitie is best;.
Jerome'sIeromes Bible, Faustus, view it well:.
The reward of sinsinne is death: that'sthats hard.

l.foreign: Si peccasse negamus, fallimur, & nulla est in nobis veritas.

sp.l: If we say that we havehaue no sinsinne,
We deceivedeceiue our selvesselues, and there'stheres no truth in usvs.
Why then belike we must sinsinne,
And so consequently die.
AyI, we must die an everlastingeuerlasting death.:
What doctrine call you this, Che sera, sera,:
What willwil be, shall be? DivinityDiuinitie, adieu.,
These MetaphysicsMetaphisickes of Magicians,
And NecromanticNegromantike booksbookes are heavenlyheauenly;
Lines, circles, scenessceanes, letters and characters,:
AyI, these are those that Faustus most desires.
O what a world of profit and delight,
Of power, of honor, of omnipotence
Is promised to the studious artisanArtizan?
All things that movemooue betweenbetweene the quiet poles
Shall beShalbe at my commandcommaund., EmperorsEmperours and Kings,
Are but obeyedobeyd in their severalseuerall provincesprouinces:
Nor can they raise the windwinde, or rend the cloudscloudes;:
But his dominion that exceedsexceedes in this,
Stretcheth as farfarre as doth the mindminde of man.
A sound magicianMagician is a mighty god:
HereHeere Faustus trytrie thy brainsbraines to gaingaine a deitydeitie.
Wagner, commend me to my dearestdeerest friends,
The GermanGermaine Valdes, and Cornelius;,
Request them earnestly to visitvisite me.
I willwil sir. exit.
Their conference will be a greater help to me,
ThanThn all my labours, plodplodde I ne'ernere so fast.
sp.stage: Enter the Goodgood AngelAngell and the Evileuill AngelAngell.

sp.speaker: Good. AngelA.
sp.l: O Faustus, lay that damned bookbooke aside,
And gaze not on it, lest it tempt thy soulsoule,
And heapheape Gods heavyheauy wrath uponvpon thy head,
ReadReade, readreade the scriptures, that is blasphemy.
Go forward, Faustus, in that famous art,
Wherein all nature'snatures treasury is containedcontaind:
Be thou on earth as JoveIoue is in the skyskie,
Lord and commandercommaunder of these elementsElements. Exeunt.
How am I glutted with conceit of this?
Shall I make spirits fetch me what I please,
ResolveResolue me of all ambiguities,
PerformPerforme what desperate enterprise I will?
I'llIle havehaue them flyflye to India for gold,
RansackRansacke the Ocean for orient pearlpearle,
And search all corners of the new found world
For pleasant fruitsfruites and princely delicates;:
I'llIle havehaue them readreade memee strangestraunge philosophyphilosophie,
And tell the secrets of all foreignforraine kings;,
I'llIle havehaue them wall all GermanyIermany with brassbrasse,
And make swift Rhine circle faire WertenbergWertenberge;
I'llIle havehaue them fill the publicpublike schoolsschooles with silkskill,.
Wherewith the students shall beshalbe bravelybrauely clad;:
I'llIle levyleuy soldierssouldiers with the coincoyne they bring,
And chase the Prince of Parma from our land,
And reignraigne sole king of all our provincesprouinces;:
Yea, stranger engines for the brunt of warwarre,
Then was the fiery keelkeele at Antwarpe'sAntwarpes bridge,
I'llIle make my servileseruile spirits to inventinuent.:
Come, GermanGermaine Valdes and Cornelius,
And make me blest with your sage conference.,
Valdes,sweetsweete Valdes, and Cornelius,
Know that your words havehaue wonwoon me at the last,
To practice magicMagicke and concealed arts:
Yet not your words onlyonely, but mine ownowne fantasyfantasie,
That will receivereceiue no object for my head,
But ruminates on necromanticNegromantique skill.,
Philosophy is odious and obscure,
Both lawLaw and physicPhisicke are for pettypettie wits;,
DivinityDiuinitie is basest of the three,
UnpleasantVnpleasant, harsh, contemptible and vilevilde,
'TisTis magicMagicke, magicMagicke that hath ravishedrauisht Mephistophilismee,
Then, gentle friends, aideayde me in this attempt.,
And I that havehaue with concise syllogismsConsissylogismes
Gravell'dGraueld the pastorsPastors of the GermanGermaine churchChurch,
And made the floweringflowring pride of WertenbergWertenberge
SwarmSwarme to my problemsProblemes, as the infernalinfernall spirits,
On sweet Musoeus when he came to hell,
Will be as cunning as Agrippa was,
Whose shadowsshadowes made all Europe honor him.
Faustus, these booksbookes thy wit and our experience
Shall make all nations to canonize usvs:,
As Indian Moores obey their Spanish Lords,
So shall the subjectssubiects of everyeuery element
Be alwaysalwaies serviceableseruiceable to usvs three,
Like lionsLyons shall they guard usvs when we please,
Like Almaine ruttersRutters with their horsemen'shorsemens stavesstaues,
Or Lapland giantsGyants trotting by our sides;,
Sometimes like women, or unweddedvnwedded maidsmaides,
Shadowing more beautybeautie in their airyayrie browsbrowes,
ThanThen in their white breasts of the queenqueene of loveLoue,:
For Venice shall they dragdregge huge Argoces,
And from America the golden fleece,
That yearlyyearely stuffsstuffes oldolde Philips treasury,
If learned Faustus will be resolute.
Valdes as resolute am I in this
As thou to liveliue;, therefore objectobiect it not.
The miraclesmyracles that magicMagicke will performperforme,
Will make thee vow to studystudie nothing else,
He that is grounded in AstrologyAstrologie,
EnrichedInricht with tongues, well seenseene in minerals,
Hath all the principles magicMagicke doth require.,
Then doubt not, (Faustus,) but to be renownedrenowmd,
And more frequented for this mystery,
Then heretofore the DelphianDolphian Oracle.
The spirits tell me they can drydrie the sea,
And fetch the treasure of all foreignforraine wrackswrackes,
AyI, all the wealth that our forefathers hid
Within the massymassie entrailsentrailes of the earth.
Then tell me, Faustus, what shallshal we three want?
Nothing, Cornelius;, O this cheerscheares my soulsoule.,
Come showshewe me some demonstrations magicalmagicall,
That I may conjure in some lustylustie grovegroue,
And havehaue these joysioyes in full possession.
Then haste thee to some solitary grovegroue,
And bearbeare wise Bacon'sBacons and Albanus'Albanus worksworkes,
The Hebrew Psalter, and Newnew Testament,
And whatsoeverwhatsoeuer else is requisiterequisit
WeWee will informenforme thee ere our conference cease.
Valdes, first let him know the words of art;,
And then, all other ceremonies learnedlearnd,
Faustus may trytrie his cunning by himselfhimselfe.
First I'llIle instruct thee in the rudiments.,
And then wilt thou be perfecter thanthen I.
Then come and dinedyne with me, and after meatmeate,
We'llWe'le canvascanuas everyeuery quiddityquidditie thereof,:
For ere I sleepsleepe I'llIle trytrie what I can do;,
This night I'llI'le conjureconiure though I die therefore.

sp.speaker: 1. ScholarSch.
sp.p:
I wonder what'swhats become of Faustus, that was
wont to make our schoolsschooles ring with, sic probo.

sp.speaker: 2. ScholarSch.
sp.p:
That shall we know, for see here comes his boy.
Enter Wagner.

sp.speaker: 1. ScholarSch.
sp.p: How now sirrahsirra, where'swheres thy mastermaister?
God in heavenheauen knowsknowes.

sp.speaker: 2.Scholar
sp.p: Why, dost not thou know?
Yes, I know, but that followsfollowes not.

sp.speaker: 1.Scholar
sp.p:
Go totoo,sirrahsirra!, leaveleaue your jestingieasting, and tell usvs where
hehee is.

That follows not necessary by force of argument,
that you being licentiate should stand upon'tvpon't, therefore ac-
knowledge your error, and be attentiveattentiue.

sp.speaker: 2.Scholar
sp.p: Why, did'stdidst thou not say thou knew'stknewst?
HaveHaue you any witnesswitnesse on't?

sp.speaker: 1.Scholar
sp.p: Yes ,sirrahsirra, I heard you.
AskAske my fellow if I be a thiefthiefe.

sp.speaker: 2.Scholar
sp.p: Well, you will not tell usvs?.

Yes sir, I will tell you, yet if you were not dunces
you would neverneuer askaske me such a question, for is not he cor-
pus naturale, and is not that mobile, then wherefore should
you askaske me such a question?: Butbut that I am by nature phleg-fleg-
maticmaticke, slowslowe to wrath, and prone to lecheryleachery, (to loveloue, I
would say),) it were not for you to come within fortyfortie footfoote of
the place of execution, although I do not doubt to see you
both hang'd the next sessionsSessions. Thus havinghauing triumphedtriumpht overouer
you, I will set my countenancecountnance like a precisionprecisian, and begin to
speakspeake thus: truly my deardeare brethren, my mastermaister is within
at dinner with Valdes and Cornelius, as this wine if it could
speakspeake, it would informenforme your worships, and so the Lord
blessblesse you, preservepreserue you, and keepkeepe you my deardeare brethren,
my deardeare brethren.
exit.

sp.speaker: 1.Scholar
sp.p:
Nay,then, I fearfeare he hasis fallenfalne into that damned art, for
which they two are infamous through the world.

Were he a stranger, and not alliedalied to me, yet should
I grievegrieue for him.: Butbut come let usvs go and informinforme the Rector,
and see if hehee by his gravegraue counselcounsaile can reclaimreclaime him.
O, but I fearfeare me nothing can reclaimreclaime him.
Yet let usvs trytrie what we can do.

sp.l: Now that the gloomy shadow of the earth,
Longing to view Orion'sOrions drizzlingdrisling looklooke,
LeapsLeapes from th'antarcticth'antartike world untovnto the skyskie,
And dimsdimmes the welkin with her pitchy breath:
Faustus, begin thine incantations,
And trytrie if devilsdiuels will obey thy hest,
Seeing thou hast prayedprayde and sacrificedsacrific'd to them.
Within this circle is Jehovah'sIehouahs name,
Forward and backward, anagrammatis'dand Agramathist,
The breviatedbreuiated names of holy Saints,
Figures of everyeuery adjunctadiunct to the heavensheauens,
And characters of signssignes and erring starsstarres,.
By which the spirits are enforcedinforst to rise.,
Then fearfeare not Faustus, but be resolute,
And trytrie the uttermostvttermost magicMagicke can performperforme.
sp.p:
Sint mihi Deidei Acherontisacherontis propitiipropitij!, Valeatvaleat numen triplex JehovaeIehouae!, Igneiignei,
aeriIaerij, aquataniAquatani spiritus, salvetesaluete!, Orientis princeps BeelzebubBelsibub, inferni
ardentis monarcha & Demigorgondemigorgon, propitiamus vos, utvt appareatapariat &
surgat MephistophilisMephastophilis., Quidqu òtud moraristumeraris?, Perper JehovamIehouam, Gehennamgehennam, &
consecratam aquam quam nunc spargo, signumquesignúmque crusis quod nunc

philis.
Enter a DevilDiuell.
sp.l: I charge thee to returnreturne and changechaunge thy shape;,
Thou art too uglyvgly to attend on me,
GoGoe and returnreturne an old Franciscan FriarFrier;,
That holy shape becomes a devildiuell best. Exit Devildiuell.
I see there'stheres virtuevertue in my heavenlyheauenly words;,
Who would not be proficient in this art?
How pliant is this MephistophilisMephastophilis?
Full of obedience and humilityhumilitie,
Such is the force of magicMagicke and my spellsspels.,
NowNo Faustus, thou art conjurerConiurer laureate
That canst commandcommaund great MephistophilisMephastophilis,
Quin regis MephistophilisMephastophilis fratris imagine.
Now, Faustus, what would'stwouldst thou havehaue me do?
I charge thee wait uponvpon me whilst I liveliue,
To do what evereuer Faustus shall commandcommaund,
Be it to make the MoonMoone drop from her spherespheare,
Or the Ocean to overwhelmouerwhelme the world.
I am a servantseruant to great Lucifer,
And may not follow thee without his leaveleaue,
No more thanthen he commandscommaunds must we performperforme.
Did not he charge thee to appearappeare to memee?
No, I came now hither of mine ownowne accord.
Did not my conjuringconiuring speeches raise thee? Speakspeake.
That was the cause, but yet per accident,
For when we hearheare one rackracke the name of God,
AbjureAbiure the scriptures, and his SaviorSauiour Christ,
WeWee flyflye, in hope to get his glorious soulsoule;,
Nor will we come unlessvnlesse he usevse such meansmeanes
Whereby he is in danger to be damneddamnd:
Therefore the shortest cut for conjuringconiuring
Is stoutly to abjure the TrinityTrinitie,
And pray devoutlydeuoutly to the Princeprince of Hellhell.
So Faustus hath already done, & holds this principle:
There is no chiefchiefe but onlyonely BeelzebubBelsibub,
To whom Faustus doth dedicate himselfhimselfe.,
This word damnation terrifies not him,
For he confounds hell in ElysiumElizium;,
His ghost be with the oldolde philosophersPhilosophers.,
But, leavingleauing these vainvaine trifles of men'smens soulssoules,
Tell me what is that Lucifer thy Lord?
Arch-regent and commandercommaunder of all spirits.
Was not that Lucifer an AngelAngell once?
Yes, Faustus, and most dearlydearely lov'dlou'd of God.
How comes it then that he is Princeprince of devilsdiuels?
O, by aspiring pride and insolence,
For which God threw him from the face of heavenheauen.
Andand what are you that liveliue with Lucifer?
UnhappyVnhappy spirits that fell with Lucifer,
ConspiredConspir'd against our God with Lucifer,
And are for evereuer damneddamnd with Lucifer.
WhereVVhere are you damneddamn'd?
In hell.
How comes it then that thou art out of hellhel?
Why this is hellhel, nor am I out of it.:
Thinkst thou that I who saw the face of God,
And tasted the eternal joysioyes of heavenheauen,
Am not tormented with ten thousand hellshels,
In being depriveddepriv'd of everlastingeuerlasting blissblisse?:
O Faustus, leaveleaue these frivolousfriuolous demandsdemaunds,
Whichwhich strike a terror to my fainting soulsoule.
What, is great MephistophilisMephastophilis so passionate,
For being depriveddeprivd of the joysioyes of heavenheauen?
LearnLearne thou of Faustus manly fortitude,
And scornscorne those joysioyes thou neverneuer shallshalt possesspossesse.
Go bearbeare those tidings to great Lucifer:,
Seeing Faustus hath incurredincurrd eternaleternall death,
By desperatedesprate thoughts against Jove'sIoues deitydeitie,:
Say, he surrenders upvp to him his soulsoule,
So he will spare him four and twenty24. yearsyeeres,
Letting him liveliue in allal voluptuousnessvoluptuousnesse,
HavingHauing thee evereuer to attend on me,
To givegiue me whatsoeverwhatsoeuer I shallshal askaske,
To telltel me whatsoeverwhatsoeuer I demanddemaund,
To slay mine enemies, and aideayde my friends,
And alwaysalwayes be obedient to my willwil.:
GoGoe and returnreturne to mighty Lucifer,
And meetmeete memee in my study at midnight,
And then resolveresolue me of thy master'smaisters mindminde.
I will, Faustus.
Had I as many soulssoules as there be starsstarres,
I'dIde givegiue them allal for MephistophilisMephastophilis.:
By him I'llIle be great EmperorEmprour of the world,
And make a bridge through the movingmoouing airayre,
To passpasse the Ocean with a band of men;,
I'llIle joinioyne the hillshils that bindbinde the AfricAffricke shore,
And make that land continent to SpainSpaine,
And both contributory to my crowncrowne.:
The EmperorEmprour shallshal not liveliue but by my leaveleaue,
Nor any Potentate of Germany.:
Now that I havehaue obtainedobtaind what I desire,
I'llIle liveliue in speculation of this artArt,
'TilTil MephistophilisMephastophilis returnreturne againagaine. Exitexit.

sp.p: SirrahSirra, boy, come hither.

How, boy? Swownsswowns boy!, I hope you havehaue seenscene ma-
ny boysboyes with such pickadevaunts as I havehaue. Boy, quotha?
TellTel me, sirrahsirra, hast thou any comingscommings in?
AyI, and goings out too, you may see else.

Alas poorpoore slaveslaue., Seesee how povertypouerty jestethiesteth in his na-
kednesskednesse., Thethe villainvilaine is bare, and out of serviceseruice, and so hun-
gry, that I know he would glue his soulsoule to the DevilDiuel for a
shoulder of mutton, though it were blood rawrawe.

How, my soulsoule to the devilDiuel for a shoulder of mut-
ton though 'tweretwere blood rawrawe? Notnot so, good friend., By'rburladie LadyI, I
had needneede havehaue it wellwel roasted, and good saucesawce to it, if I pay so
deardeere.

Wellwel, wilt thou serveserue me, and I'llIle make thee go like
Qui mihi discipulus?
How, in verse?
No, sirrahsirra, in beaten silksilke and stavesacrestaues acre.

Howhow, how, Knavesknaues acre? AyI, I thought that was allal
the land his father left him.: DoDoe yeyee hearheare?, I would be sorrysorie
to robrobbe you of your livingliuing.
SirrahSirra, I say in stavesacrestaues acre.

Oho!, Ohooho!, Stavesstaues acre!, Whywhy, then, belike, if I were
your man, I should be fullful of verminvermine.

So thou shalt, whether thou beest with me, or no.:
Butbut sirrahsirra, leaveleaue your jestingiesting, and bindbinde your selfselfe presently
untovnto me for sevenseauen yearsyeeres, or I'llIle turnturne allal the lice about thee
into familiars, and they shallshal tearteare thee in piecespeeces.

DoDoe you hearheare sir? Youyou may savesaue that labour;, they
are too familiar with me already., Swownsswowns! they are as boldbolde
with my flesh, as if they had paidpayd for my meatmeate and drinkdrinke.
Wellwel, do you hearheare sirrahsirra? Holdholde, take these guildersgilders.
GridironsGridyrons!, what be they?
Why, french crownscrownes.

MassMas, but for the name of french crownscrownes, a man
were as good havehaue as many Englishenglish counters, and what
should I do with these?

WhyVVhy, now, sirrahsirra, thou art at an hour'shoures warning,
whensoeverwhensoeuer or wheresoeverwheresoeuer the devildiuell shall fetch thee.
No, no., Herehere, take your gridirons againagaine.
Truly I'llIle none of them.
Truly but you shall.
BearBeare witnesswitnesse I gavegaue them him.
BearBeare witnesswitnesse I givegiue them you againagaine.

WellVVell, I will cause two devilsdiuels presently to fetch
thee away Baliol and Belcher.

Let your BaliolBalio and your Belcher come here, and I'llIle
knockknocke them, they were neverneuer so knockedknocht since they were de-di-
vilsuels., Saysay I should kill one of them, what would folksfolkes say? Dodo

sp.speaker: Clow.
sp.p:
Whatwhat, are they gone? Aa vengeance on them;, they

Well, sirrahsirra, follow me.

But do you hear? Ifif I should serveserue you, would you

I will teach thee to turnturne thy selfselfe to anything, to

How!? Aa Christian fellow to a dogdogge or a catcatte, a

tie wenches plackets; I'llIle be amongst them, i'faithifaith.
WellWel, sirrahsirra, come.
But, dodoe you hearheare, Wagner?
How! BaliolBalioll and Belcher.
O Lord, I pray sir, let Banio and Belcher go sleep>sleepe.

VillainVilaine, call me MasterMaister Wagner,. and let thy left

gias nostras insistere. Exitexit.

God forgiveforgiue me, he speaksspeakes Dutch fustian.: Wellwell,
I'llIle followfolow him, I'llIle serveserue him, that'sthats flat. Exitexit.

sp.l: Now, Faustus, must thou needsneedes be damneddamnd,
And canst thou not be savedsaued?
Whatwhat bootsbootes it then to thinkthinke of God or heavenheauen?
Away with such vainvaine fancies and despairdespaire:,
DespairDespaire in God, and trust in BeelzebubBelsabub.:
Now go not backwardbackeward: no, Faustus, be resolute.,
Whywhy waverestwauerest thou? O, something soundeth in mine earseares:
AbjureAbiure this magicMagicke, turnturne to God again.,
AyI, and Faustus willwil turnturne to God againagaine.,
To God? Hehe lovesloues thee not.,
The Godgod thou serv'stseruest is thine ownowne appetite,
Whereinwherein is fixedfixt the loveloue of BeelzebubBelsabub;,
To him I'llIle build an altaraltare and a church,
And offer luke warmwarme blood of new borne babes.

sp.speaker: Good Angel
sp.l: Sweet Faustus, leaveleaue that execrable art.
Contrition, prayer, repentance: what of them?

sp.speaker: Good Angel
sp.l: O, they are meansmeanes to bring thee untovnto hea-
venuen.

sp.speaker: EvilEuill Angel
sp.l: Rather illusions, fruitsfruites of lunacy,
That makes men foolish that do trust them most.

Within the bowels of these elements,

Come on, MephistophilisMephastophilis, what shall we do?

Nay, I know not., Wewe shall beshalbe cursedcurst with bell, bookbooke,
and candle.

How? bell, bookbooke, and candle, candle, bookbooke, and bell,
Forward and backward, to curse Faustus to hell.
Anon you shallshal hearheare a hoghogge grunt, a calfcalfe bleatbleate, and an
assasse braybraye, because it is SaintS. Peter'sPeters holy day.

Come, brethren, let'slets about our businessbusinesse with good

Cursed be he that tooktooke away his Holiness'holinesse wine.

Faustus and Mephistophilis beatBeate the FriarsFriers, and fling fire=worksfier-workes among

sp.speaker: Chorus
sp.l: WhenVVhen Faustus had with pleasure ta'entane the view
Of rarest things, and royal courts of kings,
HeHee stayedstayde his course, and so returned home,
Where such as bearbeare his absence, but with griefgriefe,
I meanmeane his friends and nearest companions,
Did gratulate his safetysafetie with kindkinde words,
And in their conference of what befell,
Touching his journeyiourney through the world and airayre,
They put forth questions of astrologyAstrologie,
WhichVVhich Faustus answeredanswerd with such learned skill,
As they admiredadmirde and wonderedwondred at his wit.
Now is his fame spread forth in every land;,
Amongst the rest the EmperorEmperour is one,
Carolus the fifthfift, at whose palacepallace now
Faustus is feasted 'mongstmongst his noblemen.
WhatVVhat there he did in trialtriall of his art,
I leaveleaue untoldvntold, your eyes shall see perform'dperformd

div.stage: Enter Robin the Ostler with a bookbooke in his hand.

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p:
O, this is admirable! Herehere I ha'ha stolenstolne one of doctor
Faustus'Faustus conjuringconiuring books, and i'ifaith faithI Imeane meanto tosearch searchsome some
circles for my ownowne usevse.: Nownow willwil I make allal the maidens in
our parish dance at my pleasure starkstarke naked before me, and
so by that meansmeanes I shallshal see more then ere I felt, or saw yet.

Robin, pritheeprethee come away;, there'stheres a gentlemanGentleman
tarries to havehaue his horse, and he would havehaue his things rubbedrubd

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p:
KeepKeepe out, keep out, or else you are blownblowne upvp;, you
are dismembereddismembred RalphRafe, keepkeepe out, for I am about a roaring
piecepeece of workworke.

Come, what dostdoest thou with that same bookbooke? Thouthou
canst not readreade.?

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p:
Yes, my mastermaister and mistressmistris shallshal findfinde that I can
readreade, he for his forehead, she for her privatepriuate study;, she'sshee's
borne to bearbeare with me, or else my artArt failsfailes.
Why , Robin, what bookbooke is that?

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p:
What bookbooke? Whywhy, the most intolerableintollerable bookbooke for
conjuringconiuring that ere was inventedinuented by any brimstone devildiuel.
Canst thou conjureconiure with it?

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p:
I can do allal these things easily with it: first, I can
make thee drunkdruncke with ipocrasipocrase at any taverntaberne in Europe
for nothing; that'sthats one of my conjuringconiuring worksworkes.
Our Mastermaister Parson sayssayes that'sthats nothing.

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p:
True, RalphRafe, and more RalphRafe;, if thou hast any mind
to Nan Spit, our kitchenkitchin maidmaide, then turnturne her and wind herhir
to thy ownowne use, as often as thou wilt, and at midnight.

O bravebraue Robin, shallshal I havehaue Nan Spit, and to mine
ownOwne usevse? On that condition I'llIle feedfeede thy devildiuel with horse-
bread as long as he livesliues, of free cost.

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p:
No more, sweetsweete RalphRafe, let'sletts gogoe and make cleancleane
our bootsbootes, which lie foulfoule uponvpon our handshandes, and then to our
conjuringconiuring in the devil'sdiuels name. Exeuntexeunt.

div.stage: Enter Robin and RalphRafe with a silver gobletGoblet.

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p:
Come, RalphRafe, did not I tell thee, we were for evereuer
made by this doctor Faustus' bookbooke? Ecceecce signum, here'sheeres a sim-
ple purchase for horse-keepers:, our horses shallshal eateate no hay as
long as this lasts. Enterenter the Vintner.
But Robin, here comes the vintner.

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p:
Hush, I'llIle gullgul him supernaturally.: Drawer, I
hope allal is paidpayd;, God be with you., Comecome, RalphRafe.

Soft, sir, a word with you., I must yet havehaue a gob-
let paidpayde from you ere you gogoe.

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p:
I, a goblet, RalphRafe;, I, a goblet? I scornscorne you,: and you
are but a &c. I, a goblet? Searchsearch me.
I meanmeane so, sir, with your favorfauor.

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p: How say you now?

sp.speaker: Vintner
sp.p: I must say somewhat to your fellowfelow., Youyou, sir.

Me, sir., Meme, sir., Searchsearch your fill.: Nownow, sir, you may be

sp.speaker: Vintner
sp.p: WellWel, tone of you hath this goblet about you.

You lie, Drawer;, 'tistis afore me.: Sirrahsirra you, I'llIle teach ye

Peccatum peccatorum., Here'sheeres thy goblet, good Vint-
ner.

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p:
Misericordia pro nobis., Whatwhat shallshal I dodoe? Goodgood devildiuel,

Enter to them MephistophilisMeph.

sp.speaker: Meph.
sp.p:
Vanish villainsvilaines, th'one like an apeApe, anotheran likeother like
a bearBeare, the third an assAsse, for doing this enterprise.
sp.l: Monarch of hellhel, undervnder whose blackblacke surveysuruey
Great potentatesPotentates do kneelkneele with awful fearfeare,
UponVpon whose altars thousand soulssoules do lie,
How am I vexed with these villainsvilaines charmscharmes?
From Constantinople am I hither come,
OnlyOnely for pleasure of these damned slavesslaues.

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p:
How, from Constantinople? Youyou havehaue had a great
journeyiourney., Willwil you take sixsixe pence in your purse to pay for your
supper, and be gone?

Wellwel villainsvillaines, for your presumption, I transformtransforme
thee into an apeApe, and thee into a dogDog, and so be gone. Exitexit.

sp.speaker: Rob.
sp.p:
How, into an apeApe? That'sthats bravebraue., I'llIle havehaue fine sport
with the boysboyes., I'llIle get nuts and apples enoughenow.
And I must be a dogDogge. Exeunt.exeunt.

sp.speaker: Robin
sp.p: I'faithIfaith thy head willwil neverneuer be out of the pottagepotage pot.

div.stage:
Enter EmperorEmperour, Faustus, and a Knight,
with attendantsAttendants.

sp.p:
MasterMaister Doctordoctor Faustus, I havehaue heard strange re-
port of thy knowledge in the blacke artArte, how that none in
my Empire, nor in the whole world can compare with thee,
for the rare effects of magicMagicke;: they say thou hast a familiar
spirit, by whomwhome thou canst accomplish what thou list., Thisthis,
therefore, is my request, that thou let me see some proofproofe of thy
skillskil, that mine eyeseies may be witnesses to confirmconfirme what mine
earseares havehaue heard reported, and here I swearsweare to thee, by the
honor of mine imperialImperial crowncrowne, that whateverwhat thoueuer doestthou, doest,
thou shalt be no wayswayes prejudiced or endamagedindamaged.

sp.speaker: Knight
sp.p: I'faithIfaith he lookslookes much like a conjurorconiurer.

My graciousgratious sovereignSoueraign, though I must confessconfesse
myselfmy farselfe inferiorfarre toinferior theto reportthe menreport havemen publishedhaue, andpublished, and
nothing answerable to the honor of your imperialImperial majesty,
yet for that loveloue and dutyduety bindsbindes me thereuntotherevnto, I am con-
tent to do whatsoeverwhatsoeuer your majestymaiesty shall command me.

Then, Doctordoctor Faustus, markmarke what I shall say., As
I was sometime solitary set, within my closetCloset, sundry
thoughts arose, about the honour of mine ancestorsauncestors, howhowe
they had wonwonne by prowessprowesse such exploits, gotgote such riches,
subdued so many kingdomskingdomes, as we that do succeedsucceede, or they
that shallshal hereafter possesspossesse our throne, shallshal (I fearfeare me) ne-
veruer attainattaine to that degree of high renownrenowne and great autho-
rityritie, amongstamongest which kings is Alexander the great, chiefchiefe
spectacle of the world'sworldes preeminenceprehemince,
sp.l: The bright shining of whose glorious actsactes
Lightens the world with his reflecting beamsbeames,
As when I hearheare but motion made of him,
It grievesgrieues my soulsoule I neverneuer saw the man.:
If, therefore, thou, by cunning of thine artArt,
Canst raise this man from hollow vaults below,
Wherewhere lies entombedintombde this famous conquerourConquerour,
And bring with him his beauteous paramourParamour,
Both in their right shapes, gesture, and attire
They usedvsde to wearweare during their time of life,
Thou shalt both satisfysatisfie my justiust desire,
And givegiue me cause to praise thee whilst I liveliue.

sp.p:
My graciousgratious Lord, I am ready to accomplish your
request, so farfarre forth as by art and power of my spirit I am
able to performperforme.

sp.speaker: Knight
sp.p: I'faithIfaith that'sthats justiust nothing at all.

But if it like your Grace, it is not in my abilityabilitie to
present before your eyes, the true substantialsubstantiall bodies of those
two deceased princes, which long since are consumed to dust.

sp.speaker: Knight
sp.p:
AyI, marrymary, Mastermaster Doctordoctor, now there'stheres a signsigne of grace
in you, when you will confessconfesse the truthtrueth. Asideaside.

But such spiritsspirites as can livelyliuely resemble Alexander
and his Paramour, shallshal appearappeare before your Grace, in that
manner that they best livedliu'd in, in their most flourishingflorishing estate,
which I doubt not shallshal sufficiently content your imperialImperiall
majesty.

sp.speaker: Em
sp.p: Go to, Mastermaister Doctor, let me see them presently.

sp.speaker: Knight
sp.p: Do you hearheare, Mastermaister Doctor? Youyou bring Alexander and his paramour before the Emperoremperor?
How then, sir?

sp.speaker: Kn.
sp.p: I'faithIfaith that'sthats as true as Diana turnedturnd me to a stage.

No, sir, but when Acteon died, he left the hornshornes for
you.:
MephistophilisMephastophilis, be gone. Exitexit MephistophilisMeph.

sp.speaker: Kn.
sp.p:
Nay, anand you go to conjuringconiuring, I'llIle be gone.
Exitexit KnightKn.:

I'llIle meetmeete with you anonanone for interrupting me so.:
Hereheere they are my graciousgratious Lord.
Enter MephistophilisMeph: with Alexander and his paramour.

MasterMaister Doctor, I heard this Lady while she livedliu'd
had a wart or molemoale in her necknecke., Howhow shallshal I know whether
it be so or no?

Your highnesshighnes may boldly go and see.
Exitexit AlexanderAlex.:

Sure these are no spiritsspirites, but the true substantialsubstantiall
bodies of those two deceased princes.

Will'twilt please your highnesshighnes now to send for the knight
that was so pleasantpleasent with me here of late?
One of you call him forthfoorth. Exit Attendant.

div.stage: Enter the Knight with a pairpaire of hornshornes on his head.

sp.p:
How now, sir knight? Whywhy I had thought thou
hadst beenbeene a bachelorbatcheler, but now I see thou hast a wife, that
not only givesgiues thee hornshornes, but makes thee wearweare them, feelfeele
on thy head.

sp.speaker: Knight
sp.l: Thou damned wretch, and execrable dogdogge,
Bred in the concaveconcaue of some monstrous rockrocke.:
How darestdarst thou thus abuse a gentlemanGentleman?
VillainVilaine, I say, undovndo what thou hast done.

sp.p:
O, not so fast sir;, there'stheres no haste; but, good, are you
rememberedremembred how you crossed me in my conference with the
Emperoremperour? I thinkthinke I havehaue met with you for it.

Good MasterMaister Doctor, at my entreatyintreaty release him;,
he hath done penance sufficient.

My GraciousGratious Lord, not so much for the injuryiniury hehee
offeredofferd me hereheere in your presence, as to delight you with some
mirth, hath Faustus worthily requited this injuriousiniurious knight,
which being all I desire, I am content to release him of his
hornshornes: and, sir knight, hereafter speakspeake well of scholarsScholers.:
MephistophilisMephastophilis, transformtransforme him strightstrait.
Mephistophilis removes the horns. Now my good Lord
havinghauing done my dutyduety, I humbly take my leaveleaue.

sp.speaker: emp.
sp.p:
FarewellFarewel, Mastermaister Doctor, yet ere you gogoe, expect
from me a bounteous reward. Exitexit EmperorEmperour.

sp.l: Now, MephistophilisMephastophilis, the restlessrestlesse course that time
doth runrunne with calmcalme and silent footfoote,
ShorteningShortning my daysdayes and threadthred of vitalvitall life,
Calls for the payment of my latest yearsyeares.,
sp.p:
Therefore, sweet MephistophilisMephastophilis, let usvs make haste to Wer-
tenbergtenberge.
Whatwhat, willwil you gogoe on horse backbacke, or on footfoote?

Nay, 'tiltil I am past this faire and pleasant greengreene, I'llile
walkwalke on footfoote. Enterenter a Horse-courser.

I havehaue beenbeene allal this day seeking one mastermaister Fu-
stian: massmasse, see where he is., God savesaue you, Mastermaister Doctordoctor.
What, horse-courser;, you are wellwel met.

Do you hearheare sir? I havehaue brought you forty dol-
larslers for your horse.

I cannot sellsel him so.: Ifif thou lik'stlikst him for fifty, take
him.

Alas sir, I havehaue no more;, I pray you speakspeake for
me.

I pray you let him havehaue him;, he is an honest fellowfelow,
and he has a great charge, neither wife nor childchilde.

WellWel, come givegiue me your money., Mymy boy willwil deli-
veruer him to you,: but I must telltel you one thing before you havehaue
him:, ride him not into the water at any hand.
Whywhy sir, willwil he not drinkdrinke of all waters?

O yes, he willwil drinkdrinke of allal waters, but ride him not
into the water:, ride him overouer hedge or ditch, or where thou
wilt, but not into the water.

WellWel, sir, Now am I made man foreverfor euer., I'llIle not
leaveleaue my horse for fortyfortie.: Aside. Ifif he had but the qualityqualitie of hey-hey
ding-dingding ding, hey-ding-dinghey ding ding, I'dIde make a bravebraue livingliuing on him;
hehee has a buttockbuttocke so slickslicke as an eelEle.: Wellwel, Godgod buy sir;, your
boy willwil deliverdeliuer him me.: Butbut hark ye, sir, if my horse be sick, or
ill at ease, if I bring his water to you, you'llyoule telltel me what is?
Exit Horse-courserHorsecourser

Away, you villainvillaine;: what, dostdoost thinkthinke I am a horse-
doctor? Whatwhat art thou, Faustus, but a man condemnedcondemnd to die?
sp.l: Thy fatalfatall time doth drawdrawe to finalfinall endende;,
DespairDispaire doth drivedriue distrust untovnto my thoughts:,
Confound these passions with a quiet sleepsleepe.:
Tush, Christ did call the thiefthiefe uponvpon the crossCrosse,
Then rest thee, Faustus, quiet in conceit. SleepsSleepe in his chairchaire.

sp.p:
Alas, alas!, Doctor Fustian, quothaquoth? Massa, Doctormas Doctor
Lopus was neverneuer such a Doctor., Hashas givengiuen me a purgation,
has purgedpurg'd me of fortyfortie dollarsDollers;, I shall neverneuer see them more.:
Butbut yet, like an assasse as I was, I would not be ruled by him,
for he bade me I should ride him into no water.; Nownow, I, thin-
king my horse had had some rare qualityqualitie that he would not
havehaue had me knownknowne of, I, like a venturousventrous youth, rid him in-
to the deepdeepe pond at the town'stownes endende., I was no sooner in the
middle of the pond, but my horse vanishedvanisht away, and I sat up-vp-
on a bottle of hey, neverneuer so nearneare drowning in my life.: Butbut
I'llIle seekseeke out my Doctor, and havehaue my fortyfortie dollarsdollers againagaine,
or I'llIle make it the dearest horse.: O, yonder is his snipper-snipper
snapper, do you hearheare? Youyou, hey, passpasse, where's your
mastermaister?

Whywhy sir, what would you? Youyou cannot speakspeake
with him.
But I willwil speakspeake with him.
Why, he'shee's fast asleepasleepe;, come some other time.

I'llIle speakspeake with him now, or I'llIle breakbreake his glass-glasse-
windowswindowes about his earseares.
I tell thee he has not slept this eight nights.

And he havehaue not slept this eight weeksweekes I'llIle speakspeake
with him.
See where he is fast asleepasleepe.

AyI, this is he;, God savesaue ye Mastermaister Doctordoctor, Mastermaister
Doctordoctor, Mastermaister Doctordoctor Fustian, fortyfortie dollarsdollers, fortyfortie dollarsdollers
for a bottle of hey.
Why, thou seest he hearsheares thee not.

So, ho, ho;: so, ho, ho. HollarsHallow in his eareare.
No, will you not wake? I'llIle make you wake ere I gogoe.
PullsPull Faustushim by the leglegge, and pullspull it away.
Alas, I am undonevndone!, Whatwhat shall I do?:

O, my leglegge, my leglegge, helphelpe MephistophilisMephastophilis, call the
officersOfficers, my leglegge, my leglegge.
Come, villainvillaine, to the Constable.

O Lord sir, let me gogoe, and I'llIle giveglue you fortyfortie dol-
larslers more.
Where be they?

I havehaue none about me., Comecome to my ostryOastrie, and I'llIle
givegiue them you.
Be gone quickly. Horse-courserHorsecourser runsrunnes away.

What, is he gone? Farewellfarwel he., Faustus has his leglegge
againagaine, and the Horse-courserHorsecourser I take it, a bottle of hey for his
labour.; Wellwel, this tricktricke shallshal cost him fortyfortie dollarsdollers more.
Enter Wagner.
How now, Wagner;, what's the newsnewes with thee?

Sir, the Duke of Vanholt doth earnestly entreatentreate
your company.

The Duke of Vanholt! an honourable gentleman,
to whom I must be no niggard of my cunning., Comecome, Me-
phistophilisphastophilis, let's away to him. Exeuntexeunt.

div.stage:
Enter to them the Duke of Vanholt and the DuchessDutches;,
the Duke speaksspeakes.

sp.p:
BelieveBeleeue me, Mastermaister Doctor, this merriment hath
much pleased me.

My graciousgratious lordLord, I am glad it contents you so
wellwel.: Butbut it may be, madamMadame, you take no delight in this;, I
havehaue heard that great bellied women do long for some dain-
ties or other., Whatwhat is it, madamMadame? Telltell me, and you shallshal havehaue
it.

sp.speaker: Dutch.
sp.p:
ThanksThankes, good Mastermaister Doctordoctor,
And for I see your courteouscurteous intent to pleasure me, I willwil not
hide from you the thing my heart desires, and were it nownowe
summer, as it is JanuaryIanuary, and the dead time of the winter, I
would desire no better meatmeate then a dish of ripe grapes.

Alas, madamMadame, that'sthats nothing., MephistophilisMephastophilis, be
gone: Exitexit MephistophilisMeph. Werewere it a greater thing thanthen this, so
it would content you, you should havehaue it. Enterenter MephistophilisMephasto : with the grapes.
Herehere they be, madam;, wil'twilt please you taste
on them.

BelieveBeleeue me, Mastermaster Doctor, this makes me wonder
aboveaboue the rest, that being in the dead time of winter, and in
the month of JanuaryIanuary, how you shouldshuld come by these grapes.

If it like your Gracegrace, the yearyeere is divideddiuided into twotwoo
circles overouer the whole worldworlde, that when it is hereheere winter
with usvs, in the contrary circle it is summer with them, as in
India, Saba, and farther countries in the East;, and by means
of a swift spirit that I havehaue, I had them brought hither, as ye
see, how do you like them madamMadame?, Bebe they good?

sp.speaker: Dut.
sp.p:
BelieveBeleeue me, MasterMaister Doctordoctor, they be the best grapes
that e'erere I tasted in my life before.
I am glad they content you so, madamMadam.

Come, madamMadame, let usvs in, where you must wellwel re-
ward this learned man for the great kindnesskindnes he hath showedshewd
to you.

sp.speaker: Dut.
sp.p:
And so I willwil my Lord, and whilst I liveliue,
restRest beholding for this courtesycurtesie.
I humbly thankthanke your Grace.

Come, Mastermaister Doctor, follow usvs, and receivereceiue your
reward. Exeuntexeunt.

sp.l: I thinkthinke my mastermaister meansmeanes to die shortly,
For he hath givengiuen to me allal his goodsgoodes,
And yet me thinksthinkes, if that death were nearneere,
He would not banquet, and carousecarowse, and swill
Amongst the studentsStudents, as eveneuen now he doth,
Whowho are at supper with such belly-cheerbelly-cheere,
As Wagner nevernere beheld in all his life.
See where they come.: Belikebelike the feast is ended.

div.stage: Enter Faustus, with two or three ScholarsSchollers

sp.speaker: 1. ScholarsSch.
sp.p:
MasterMaister Doctor Faustas, since our conference a-
bout faire ladiesLadies, which was the beautiful'stbeutifulst in all the world,
we havehaue determined with our selvesselues, that Helen of Greece
was the admirabl'stadmirablest Lady that evereuer livedliued.: Thereforetherefore, Mastermaster
Doctor, if you willwil do usvs that favorfauor, as to let usvs see that peer-peere-
lesslesse Dame of Greece, whomwhome allal the world admires for ma-
jestyiesty, wewee should thinkthinke our selvesselues much beholding untovnto
you.

Gentlemen, for that I know your friendship is un-vn-
fained, and Faustus customcustome is not to denydenie the justiust requests
of those that wish him well, you shall behold that peerlesspearelesse
dame of Greece, no otherwiseotherwaies for pomppompe and majestymaiestie, then
when sir Paris crossedcrost the seas with her. and brought the spoilsspoiles
to rich Dardania. Be silent then, for danger is in words.

div.stage: MusicMusicke sounds, and Helen passeth overouer the stageStage.

sp.speaker: 2. ScholarSch.
sp.l: Too simple is my wit to tell her praise,
Whom all the world admires for majestymaiestie.

sp.speaker: 3. ScholarSch.
sp.l: No marvelmaruel thoughtho the angry GreeksGreekes pursuedpursude
With tentenne yearsyeares warwarre the rape of such a queenqueene,
Whose heavenlyheauenly beauty passeth all compare.
Since we havehaue seenscene the pride of nature'snatures worksworkes,
And onlyonely paragonParagon of excellence, Enter an Oldold Manman.
Let usvs depart, and for this glorious deed
Happy and blest be Faustus evermoreeuermore.

Gentlemen, Farewellfarwel, the same I wish to you.
Exeunt ScholarsSchollers.

sp.speaker: Old .Man
sp.l: Ah, Doctor Faustus, that I might prevailpreuaile,
To guide thy steps untovnto, the way of life,
By which sweetsweete path thou maist attainattaine the goalgole
That shall conduct thee to celestial rest.
BreakBreake heart, drop bloodbloud, and mingle it with tearsteares,
TearsTeares falling from repentant heavinessheauinesse
Of thy most vilevilde and loathsome filthinessfilthinesse,
The stench whereof corrupts the inward soulsoule
With such flagitious crimes of heinous sinssinnes,
As no commiseration may expel,
But mercymercie, Faustus, of thy SaviorSauiour sweetsweete,
Whose bloodbloud alone must wash away thy guilt.

sp.l: Ah Faustus,
Now hast thou but one bare hourhower to liveliue,

Stand stillstil you evereuer movingmoouing spheres of heavenheauen,
